citations.narrative:
Multiple outdoor enclosures have not been constructed of appropriate material or maintained in good repair to protect the
animals from injury.
** On February 25, 2026, around mid-day, an adult male goat was found deceased with his head entrapped in a hole in
the front of his shelter. The shelters in his outdoor enclosure, which currently house 10 goats, 7 sheep, and one llama, are
constructed from outdoor tent material reinforced by plywood panels along the sides. The front of the tent is not reinforced
and was hanging down that day to be used as a windbreak. According to employees, the animal appeared normal on his
morning checks and no obvious tears or holes were visible. Employees suspect the goat used his head to rip or enlarge a
small hole in the flap. This shelter was not constructed of such material and strength as appropriate for the animal, and
lead to the animal's death.
**On Friday, March 13, 2026 a gust of wind blew the food storage tent into the camel's outdoor enclosure. On March 16
(three days later) the inspector observed the tent had not been moved, but maintenance employees took measures to
secure the tent to the enclosure, preventing it from blowing away further. There are several loose, rusty nails and other
sharp pieces of metal that are attached to the tent in the enclosure. These items could injure the camel.
The facilities must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The
outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from
injury and to contain the animals.
Correction date: April 1, 2026.

The food and bedding for the hoofstock is not being stored adequately.
The majority of the food, hay, and straw for these animals is currently stored in a shed with no door. The inspector
observed snow on the floor, on some straw, and on bins of food. Employees report on Friday, March 13, a tent used to
store these items blew away, and the items were moved into this shed. However, a door or similarly functioning windbreak
was not put in place when the items were moved.
The bags of wood shavings used for hoofstock bedding are being stored outdoors and exposed to the elements such as
rain and snow. Employees report there is no available indoor space to currently store this bedding during the facility's
renovations.
Storing food and bedding in a manner that does not protect from the elements can lead to deterioration or molding of
these materials. This can affect animal health.
Supplies of food and bedding shall be stored in facilities which adequately protect such supplies against deterioration,
molding, or contamination by vermin.
Correction date: April 16, 2026.

An adult llama faced a severe injury due to inadequate drainage in an outdoor enclosure.
On December 19, 2025, an adult llama slipped and fell on ice in his outdoor enclosure. The fall resulted in two leg
fractures and the animal was subsequently euthanized. Employees hypothesized the animal was leaving the shelter to
access the water bowl across the yard, which required the animal to walk across the ice.
On the day of inspection (March 16, 2026), the inspector noticed several enclosures with inadequate drainage.
**The enclosure that housed the deceased llama currently still houses 10 goats, 7 sheep, and an llama. The ground was
wet with snow, slush, and mud, with several shallow puddles. The water bowl is placed at the opposite end of the
enclosure from the shelters, requiring the animals to walk through the mud and snow to access water. The inspector
observed several animals demonstrating difficulty navigating the terrain as their hooves intermittently sunk into mud. The
animals' shelters appear to be the only dry area for the animals. Employees report this enclosure has been similarly
"muddy, snowy, or frozen" since the winter, including when the llama slipped on the ice.
** An enclosure housing 38 goats, one alpaca, and 13 sheep also does not contain dry area outside of the animals' three
shelters. Most of the ground is area is muddy and slushy. There is a large puddle just outside the entrance to one of the
shelters that appears at least one foot deep. As the inspector walked through the enclosure, occasionally the inspector's
boot would sink into a puddle or mud pit that was not visible from the surface. Animals demonstrated difficulty navigating
the terrain.
** An enclosure housing 4 adult cows also does not appear to contain dry area outside of the animals' two shelters. The
inspector observed the enclosure covered in a thin layer of snow with pitted imprints from the animals' hooves. The
animals' bottom 6 inches of hooves appeared muddy. The ground immediately outside the animals' shelter contained
puddles and pits of mud about 12 inches deep.
Inadequate drainage led to the llama's serious injury. Furthermore, the current moist, muddy conditions of the enclosures
described can result in foot and hoof problems in these animals or another injury.
The facility must ensure that a suitable method is provided to rapidly eliminate excess water and provide adequate
drainage, so that the animals do not have to stand in mud or walk across ice to access drinking water.
Correction date: April 1, 2026.

Employees report that the current level of staffing and training at the facility prevent employees from maintaining at a
professionally acceptable level of husbandry practices. Two examples of such are the following:
** There are not sufficient animal care or maintenance employees to keep the outdoor enclosures dry. The outdoor
enclosures require frequent addition of dry, absorbent material to prevent mud, snow, and ice from accumulating.
** There are not sufficient employees to maintain the hooves the hoofstock (20 sheep, 51 goats, 4 cows, one llama, and
one alpaca) on a regular basis. It takes several employees' involvement to trim the hooves of the larger hoofstock (llama,
alpaca) with the current resources the facility has available for hoof trims. The employees report intermittently overgrown
hooves as a result of this.
Lack of sufficient number of adequately trained employees can lead to health and welfare impacts on the animals.
A sufficient number of adequately trained employees shall be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level of
husbandry practices set forth in this subpart.
Correction date: April 15, 2026.
This is a continuation of the report for the routine inspection conducted on March 16, 2026 and contains the remaining
non-compliances found during the inspection. The inspection was conducted with facility representatives.
The exit briefing for the items included on this report was conducted with a facility representative on March 17, 2026.
n

narrative:
3.125(a)  Critical
   
Facilities, general. 
Multiple outdoor enclosures have not been constructed of appropriate material or maintained in good repair to protect the 
animals from injury.  
 
** On February 25, 2026, around mid-day, an adult male goat was found deceased with his head entrapped in a hole in 
the front of his shelter. The shelters in his outdoor enclosure, which currently house 10 goats, 7 sheep, and one llama, are 
constructed from outdoor tent material reinforced by plywood panels along the sides. The front of the tent is not reinforced 
and was hanging down that day to be used as a windbreak. According to employees, the animal appeared normal on his 
morning checks and no obvious tears or holes were visible. Employees suspect the goat used his head to rip or enlarge a 
small hole in the flap. This shelter was not constructed of such material and strength as appropriate for the animal, and 
lead to the animal's death.  
 
**On Friday, March 13, 2026 a gust of wind blew the food storage tent into the camel's outdoor enclosure. On March 16 
(three days later) the inspector observed the tent had not been moved, but maintenance employees took measures to 
secure the tent to the enclosure, preventing it from blowing away further. There are several loose, rusty nails and other 
sharp pieces of metal that are attached to the tent in the enclosure. These items could injure the camel.  
 
The facilities must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The 
outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from 
injury and to contain the animals. 
 
Correction date: April 1, 2026. 
3.125(c) 
   
Facilities, general. 
The food and bedding for the hoofstock is not being stored adequately.  
 
The majority of the food, hay, and straw for these animals is currently stored in a shed with no door. The inspector 
observed snow on the floor, on some straw, and on bins of food. Employees report on Friday, March 13, a tent used to 
store these items blew away, and the items were moved into this shed. However, a door or similarly functioning windbreak

was not put in place when the items were moved. 
 
The bags of wood shavings used for hoofstock bedding are being stored outdoors and exposed to the elements such as 
rain and snow. Employees report there is no available indoor space to currently store this bedding during the facility's 
renovations.  
 
Storing food and bedding in a manner that does not protect from the elements can lead to deterioration or molding of 
these materials. This can affect animal health. 
 
Supplies of food and bedding shall be stored in facilities which adequately protect such supplies against deterioration, 
molding, or contamination by vermin. 
 
Correction date: April 16, 2026. 
3.127(c)  Critical
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
An adult llama faced a severe injury due to inadequate drainage in an outdoor enclosure.  
 
On December 19, 2025, an adult llama slipped and fell on ice in his outdoor enclosure. The fall resulted in two leg 
fractures and the animal was subsequently euthanized. Employees hypothesized the animal was leaving the shelter to 
access the water bowl across the yard, which required the animal to walk across the ice.  
 
On the day of inspection (March 16, 2026), the inspector noticed several enclosures with inadequate drainage.  
 
**The enclosure that housed the deceased llama currently still houses 10 goats, 7 sheep, and an llama. The ground was 
wet with snow, slush, and mud, with several shallow puddles. The water bowl is placed at the opposite end of the 
enclosure from the shelters, requiring the animals to walk through the mud and snow to access water. The inspector 
observed several animals demonstrating difficulty navigating the terrain as their hooves intermittently sunk into mud. The

"muddy, snowy, or frozen" since the winter, including when the llama slipped on the ice. 
 
** An enclosure housing 38 goats, one alpaca, and 13 sheep also does not contain dry area outside of the animals' three 
shelters. Most of the ground is area is muddy and slushy. There is a large puddle just outside the entrance to one of the 
shelters that appears at least one foot deep. As the inspector walked through the enclosure, occasionally the inspector's

** An enclosure housing 4 adult cows also does not appear to contain dry area outside of the animals' two shelters. The

animals' bottom 6 inches of hooves appeared muddy. The ground immediately outside the animals' shelter contained 
puddles and pits of mud about 12 inches deep.

Inadequate drainage led to the llama's serious injury. Furthermore, the current moist, muddy conditions of the enclosures 
described can result in foot and hoof problems in these animals or another injury. 
 
The facility must ensure that a suitable method is provided to rapidly eliminate excess water and provide adequate 
drainage, so that the animals do not have to stand in mud or walk across ice to access drinking water.  
 
Correction date: April 1, 2026. 
3.132 
   
Employees. 
Employees report that the current level of staffing and training at the facility prevent employees from maintaining at a 
professionally acceptable level of husbandry practices. Two examples of such are the following: 
 
** There are not sufficient animal care or maintenance employees to keep the outdoor enclosures dry. The outdoor 
enclosures require frequent addition of dry, absorbent material to prevent mud, snow, and ice from accumulating. 
** There are not sufficient employees to maintain the hooves the hoofstock (20 sheep, 51 goats, 4 cows, one llama, and 
one alpaca) on a regular basis. It takes several employees' involvement to trim the hooves of the larger hoofstock (llama, 
alpaca) with the current resources the facility has available for hoof trims. The employees report intermittently overgrown 
hooves as a result of this.  
 
Lack of sufficient number of adequately trained employees can lead to health and welfare impacts on the animals. 
 
A sufficient number of adequately trained employees shall be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level of 
husbandry practices set forth in this subpart.  
 
Correction date: April 15, 2026. 
 
This is a continuation of the report for the routine inspection conducted on March 16, 2026 and contains the remaining 
non-compliances found during the inspection. The inspection was conducted with facility representatives.  
 
The exit briefing for the items included on this report was conducted with a facility representative on March 17, 2026.

species.common: DOMESTIC PIG / POTBELLY PIG / MICRO PIG
